output:
# MONOVERDICT and SPECTRA: Resolving the Pooled Negation Verdict into a Per-Phenomenon, Partially-Pooled Competence Field with Exact Collapse

A research proposal in the "5q" format, developed from an analysis of the Introduction to
Truong, Baldwin, Verspoor and Cohn (2023), "Language models are not naysayers: An analysis of
language models on negation benchmarks" [1]. The proposal applies a representational-impossibility
and exact-collapse design discipline: identify a single pooled scalar that a source method or
analysis applies uniformly while it is blind to an observable, heterogeneity-bearing covariate,
prove the scalar cannot equal the non-constant per-unit optimum, and replace it with a structured,
monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map over that covariate that provably collapses back to
the source scalar at its default setting.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 Negation as a stumbling block, and what the source paper does

Negation is a core, high-frequency linguistic phenomenon that remains a persistent failure mode
for modern language models (LMs) [1, 3, 4, 8]. The reasons are well rehearsed: negated statements
are underrepresented in pretraining corpora because speakers prefer affirmative expression [4]; the
surface forms of negation are extraordinarily diverse across domains [1, 14, 15]; and negation
interacts with other phenomena -- quantifiers, gradable adjectives ("not unattractive" does not
imply "attractive"), negative-polarity licensing ("any", "either", "yet") [16, 17], and downward
entailment ("a man does not own a dog" does not entail "a man does not own an animal") [10].

The source paper [1] is an analysis paper. It does not propose a new model; it measures how
auto-regressive LMs perform on negation-focused benchmarks, grouping them into three categories
that correspond to three negation-reasoning abilities: (1) sensitivity to negation through cloze
(fill-in-the-blank) completion of factual statements, instantiated by a negated version of LAMA
[2] (the MKR-NQ / negated-query setting in the lineage of negated and misprimed probes [3, 5]);
(2) lexical semantics of negation through antonym/synonym classification; and (3) the ability to
reason with negation through natural language inference (NLI). Its headline empirical claim --
"Finding 1: Larger LMs are more insensitive to negation" -- is an inverse-scaling statement [26, 27]:
across a model-size sweep, the pooled negation score does not improve, and on several benchmarks
worsens, with scale. The paper further reports that instruction fine-tuning (FLAN-style models
[23, 24], InstructGPT [25]) partially reverses this trend, consistent with the broader observation
that inverse-scaling curves can become U-shaped under instruction tuning [27]. The source further
notes that a small-scale probe of ChatGPT showed stronger behavior on challenging negation samples,
so its conclusions may not transfer to the newest chat-tuned models -- an observation echoed by
consistency analyses of ChatGPT under negation and related perturbations [7], and one that a
per-phenomenon account (rather than a single pooled verdict per model) is far better placed to track.

### 1.2 How negation competence is currently measured

The dominant evaluation protocol -- in the source paper and in the surrounding literature -- reduces
a model's negation ability to a small number of pooled, benchmark-level scalars:

- a per-benchmark accuracy (or an affirmative-minus-negated gap) averaged over every example in the
  benchmark, and
- a single inverse-scaling trend (one slope, or one monotone-decreasing curve) fit over model size
  to that pooled accuracy.

This pooled-scalar protocol is shared by the foundational probing studies (negated LAMA and negated
probes [2, 3], the NEG-136 psycholinguistic diagnostic [4], negated-prompt case studies [5]) and by
the inverse-scaling and emergent-abilities literature [22, 26, 27], which characterize a task by a
single curve over scale. The verdict is therefore monolithic: a model "is" or "is not" sensitive to
negation, summarized by one number per benchmark and one slope per benchmark.

### 1.3 The heterogeneity that the pooled scalar hides

Negation is not one phenomenon but a typology. The source paper's own Introduction enumerates many
distinct forms: explicit lexical negation ("not", "no", "never"); morphological / affixal negation
("un-", "in-", "non-", "-less"); implicit or inherent negation ("deny", "absence of", or the
clinical "normal chest x-ray" which implies the absence of an abnormality); domain-specific acronym
negation (clinical "NAD", no abnormality detected [18]); negation under negative-polarity-item
licensing [16, 17]; negation composed with quantifiers; negation composed with gradable adjectives;
and negation interacting with downward entailment [10]. A growing family of fine-grained negation
benchmarks makes these distinctions explicit -- MoNLI for lexical-entailment-and-negation NLI [10],
CONDAQA for contrastive negation reading comprehension [11], ScoNe for scoped negation [13], the
NaN-NLI suite for sub-clausal negation [15], and the large "This is not a Dataset" benchmark [12] --
and corpus analyses confirm that negation is both underrepresented and, where present, highly
varied across NLU corpora [8, 9]. The raw materials for a per-phenomenon view of negation competence
therefore already exist; current practice pools them away into a single number.

### 1.4 Empirical-Bayes partial pooling and item-level evaluation

Two methodological traditions are directly relevant. First, empirical-Bayes / hierarchical partial
pooling (Stein estimation and its empirical-Bayes interpretation [32]; multilevel models [33]) is the
standard statistical remedy when one wants per-group estimates from groups of very different sizes:
data-rich groups are trusted near their raw estimate, while data-poor groups are shrunk toward the
global mean, minimizing total estimation risk. Second, item-response-theory (IRT) and item-informativeness
approaches to NLP evaluation [30, 31] move beyond a single accuracy to latent per-item or per-model
ability parameters, and behavioral / subpopulation evaluation (CheckList [28], worst-group / Group
DRO objectives [29]) measures performance on targeted slices rather than the pooled mean. Crucially,
none of these has been turned onto the negation typology as a covariate: IRT estimates a single
latent ability pooled over phenomena, and subpopulation methods report raw (unshrunk) slice accuracies
without an exact-collapse guarantee back to the reported pooled negation gauge.

### 1.5 Notation and glossary (used throughout)

- m: a language model; x_m: a scalar size / scale covariate for m (for example log parameter count).
- B: a negation benchmark (cloze, lexical, or NLI category of [1]).
- y_i in {0, 1}: the correctness of model m on example i of B (the per-example output the benchmark
  already produces).
- k: a negation-phenomenon stratum (for example "morphological", "implicit", "NPI-licensing").
- n_k: the number of examples in stratum k; N = sum_k n_k.
- c_k_raw(m) = (1 / n_k) sum_{i in k} y_i: the raw per-stratum accuracy.
- A_pool(m, B) = (1 / N) sum_i y_i = sum_k (n_k / N) c_k_raw(m): the source's pooled per-benchmark
  metric, written as a weighted average of the raw per-stratum accuracies (an algebraic identity).
- c_global(m): the pooled (fully shrunk) competence prior, equal to A_pool(m, B) in the simplest form.
- r_k: a reliability / precision covariate for stratum k (precision = inverse sampling variance of
  c_k_raw), monotone increasing in n_k.
- lambda(r_k) = tau^2 / (tau^2 + var_k): the empirical-Bayes shrinkage weight, monotone increasing
  in r_k (and thus in n_k); var_k is the sampling variance of c_k_raw; tau^2 is the between-stratum
  variance fit by marginal maximum likelihood.
- c_k_tilde(m) = lambda(r_k) c_k_raw(m) + (1 - lambda(r_k)) c_global(m): the shrunk per-stratum
  competence field (the analysis product).
- beta_k: the per-stratum inverse-scaling slope (change in c_k with x_m); beta_k ~ N(beta_global, omega^2).
- gamma: a competence threshold; M_gamma: the "hard-phenomenon mass" = fraction of benchmark weight
  whose c_k_tilde lies at or below gamma, with significance assessed against a homogeneous-competence
  permutation null.
- C1-C4: the four novelty-wedge clauses (Section 2.6).
- E_collapse: the exact-collapse gate (Section 2.5).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 2. Key Findings and Analysis

### 2.1 The gap: MONOVERDICT (a monolithic negation verdict)

We name the gap MONOVERDICT: the field renders a single, monolithic verdict on a model's negation
ability -- one pooled accuracy A_pool(m, B) per benchmark, and one inverse-scaling slope beta_global
per benchmark -- where in truth negation competence is a per-phenomenon field {c_k(m)} indexed by the
negation typology of Section 1.3, and the scaling behavior is a per-phenomenon field {beta_k}.

The representational argument is exact. A_pool(m, B) is a single scalar (a weighted mean of the
c_k_raw). A scalar mean cannot distinguish a model whose competence is uniform-moderate across all
phenomena from a model that is strong on explicit lexical negation (the high-frequency majority of
most benchmarks) but fails on the implicit / scope / NPI-licensing / downward-entailment tail,
whenever the two profiles share the same weighted mean. Formally, the map from a competence field
{c_k} to its pooled mean is many-to-one; infinitely many distinct fields collapse to the same scalar.
Therefore no single pooled number can equal, or recover, the non-constant per-phenomenon optimum
profile. The same holds for scaling: one global slope beta_global cannot equal a non-constant
per-phenomenon slope field {beta_k} when different negation phenomena scale differently with model
size.

MONOVERDICT is the diagnostic-layer analogue of a single global scalar applied uniformly: the pooled
metric is a constant function over the phenomenon index k, and a constant function is representationally
incapable of expressing a non-constant target field.

### 2.2 Why the harm is SILENT under pooled metrics

The damage from MONOVERDICT is not merely lost resolution; it is an active risk of a wrong scientific
conclusion, and it is silent under exactly the metrics the field reports.

(a) Cancellation in the mean. Suppose model A is uniformly mediocre (c_k = 0.75 for all k) and model B
is excellent on the explicit-negation majority (c = 0.85 on 80% of items) but near-chance on the
implicit / scope / licensing tail (c = 0.35 on 20%, so 0.85 x 0.8 + 0.35 x 0.2 = 0.75). Both report
the same pooled A_pool = 0.75,
so the pooled verdict declares them equivalent while their deployment-relevant behavior on hard
negation differs sharply. The tail failure is averaged away by the majority.

(b) Simpson-style reversal of the headline finding. The inverse-scaling claim is a single pooled
slope. But the pooled slope is a size-weighted blend of per-phenomenon slopes. It is entirely
possible -- and is the central empirical hypothesis of this proposal -- that the pooled
"larger LMs are more insensitive to negation" trend [1] is carried by a subset of phenomena (for
example factual cloze, where scale amplifies the affirmative-prior bias that negation must overturn
[3, 5]) while other phenomena (for example lexical antonym/synonym, or certain NLI subtypes) are flat
or positively scaling. A single slope cannot reveal this and can invert the per-phenomenon truth, in
the same way that an aggregate trend can reverse a within-group trend (Simpson's paradox). A monolithic
"LLMs cannot do negation" verdict drawn from a pooled slope may therefore be partly an artifact of
mixing heterogeneous phenomena.

(c) The blindness is structural, not incidental. Because A_pool is literally the mean of the c_k_raw,
the over- and under-competent phenomena enter with opposite sign relative to the mean and partially
cancel by construction; the pooled bootstrap that the field uses to put error bars on A_pool tests
only the mean and inherits the same blindness. The gap is undetectable without a phenomenon-resolved,
partially-pooled diagnostic and falsifiers that a pooled analysis cannot run.

### 2.3 The harm SCALES with heterogeneity and VANISHES under homogeneity

The severity of MONOVERDICT is governed by the heterogeneity of the benchmark's phenomenon mixture.
When competence is spread widely across phenomena (high variance / bimodality of {c_k}, or a large
spread of {beta_k}), the pooled scalar is maximally misleading and the hidden tail is largest. When
the benchmark is homogeneous -- a single negation phenomenon, or a uniform competence field where
c_k = c_global for all k -- the pooled mean equals every per-phenomenon value and the field carries no
additional information. This is the diagnostic-domain instance of the recurring "harm scales with
heterogeneity, vanishes under homogeneity" structure, and it directly motivates both the monotone-dose
falsifier (gains grow with injected phenomenon-competence spread) and the homogeneous-null falsifier
(no spurious structure on a genuinely uniform benchmark) of Section 3, Q5.

### 2.4 Binding obstacle: metric blindness

Of the obstacles to closing this gap (Section 3, Q3), the binding one is metric blindness. The pooled
accuracy and the single inverse-scaling slope average away the per-phenomenon competence and the
per-phenomenon scaling sign; the field's own per-benchmark bootstrap shares the blindness because it
too summarizes the mean. Without (i) a phenomenon-stratified, partially-pooled estimator, (ii)
covariate-scramble, homogeneous-null and monotone-dose falsifiers, and (iii) a non-circular downstream
validation, the gap is invisible -- which is precisely why the field has been comfortable issuing a
single monolithic verdict on negation.

### 2.5 The method: SPECTRA, and its exact collapse

We name the method SPECTRA (Stratified Partially-pooled Empirical-bayes Competence esTimation
Resolving negation Abilities). SPECTRA promotes the pooled negation scalar to a per-phenomenon
competence field, estimated so that small, noisy phenomenon strata are handled correctly, and so that
the original pooled number is reproduced exactly.

Step 1 -- Typing covariate (observable, leakage-free). Assign each benchmark example i to a negation
phenomenon stratum k using only the linguistic form of the input, never the gold label and never the
model's correctness y_i. The typing uses existing cue/scope annotations where available (the negation
benchmarks of [10, 11, 12, 13, 15] and cue/scope corpora carry these), and otherwise a rule-based
negation-cue detector or an off-the-shelf negation parser [14]. Strata include: explicit lexical,
morphological/affixal, implicit/inherent, clinical-acronym [18], NPI-licensing [16, 17], negation +
quantifier, negation + gradable adjective, downward-entailment [10], double/multiple negation, plus
orthogonal axes (scope length, cue-target distance). Because the covariate is a function of the input
alone, it is computable at analysis time with no model retraining and no held-out leakage.

Step 2 -- Reliability covariate. For each stratum compute r_k, the precision (inverse sampling
variance) of c_k_raw, monotone increasing in the stratum count n_k (Wilson / Beta-Binomial variance).
Small phenomena -- exactly the hard tail that is rare in most benchmarks -- have low r_k.

Step 3 -- Partial pooling (the structured monotone map). Form the empirical-Bayes shrinkage estimate
c_k_tilde(m) = lambda(r_k) c_k_raw(m) + (1 - lambda(r_k)) c_global(m), with the monotone shrinkage
weight lambda(r_k) = tau^2 / (tau^2 + var_k) increasing in r_k. Data-rich strata stay near their raw
estimate; data-poor strata shrink toward c_global. The between-stratum variance tau^2 is fit by
marginal maximum likelihood across strata. The deployable default is a single shared map (one tau);
an optional refinement fits bin-level maps across coarse phenomenon families. This is the structured,
hierarchically partially-pooled estimator [32, 33]: not a single global scalar (the pooled metric),
and not the unshrunk, noisy per-phenomenon raw accuracies.

Step 4 -- Per-phenomenon scaling. Replace the single global slope with a hierarchical random-slope
model beta_k ~ N(beta_global, omega^2) fit jointly over the model-size sweep, yielding a per-phenomenon
scaling-sign map (which phenomena scale inversely, flatly, or positively with size) with the small
strata again partially pooled toward beta_global.

Step 5 -- Derived diagnostics. From the field, report: the negation-competence profile {c_k_tilde};
the hard-phenomenon mass M_gamma (fraction of weight with c_k_tilde at or below gamma, tested against
a homogeneous-competence permutation null); the per-phenomenon scaling-sign map; and an optional
scalar Negation Evaluability Index summarizing how much of a benchmark's verdict rests on phenomena a
model actually handles.

Exact collapse (the core safety property). SPECTRA is an additive overlay: it never alters the reported
pooled number. It retains the raw per-example correctness {y_i} and the raw per-stratum accuracies
{c_k_raw}. The strata used for the collapse are a SINGLE mutually exclusive and exhaustive partition of
the benchmark's examples (each example assigned to exactly one primary phenomenon stratum); the orthogonal
axes of Step 1 and Q3-O4 -- scope length, cue-target distance -- are reported only as separate marginal or
cross-classified analyses and are never folded into the collapse partition. The collapse operator A_pool
that recombines the RAW per-stratum accuracies with their natural weights then returns the source metric
identically, because it is the same sum regrouped:
sum_k (n_k / N) c_k_raw(m) = (1 / N) sum_i y_i = A_pool(m, B). This is an algebraic identity, not an
approximation, and -- critically -- it uses the raw materials, never the shrunk field c_k_tilde, so the
shrinkage can never corrupt the reproduced number. The gate is

  E_collapse = | sum_k (n_k / N) c_k_raw(m) - A_pool_reported(m, B) | <= 1e-6

(floating-point tolerance only), checked in continuous integration. The same guarantee extends to any
source metric that is a fixed linear functional of per-example correctness -- micro-accuracy and the
affirmative-minus-negated gap both are -- through the corresponding linear reaggregation operator; for a
macro-averaged metric the collapse operator uses the source's own macro weights. The exact-collapse
contract is therefore defined on the reported pooled METRIC, not on the inverse-scaling slope. For the
scaling component we make the weaker, correct claim: the full-pooling limit omega -> 0 of the random-slope
model is a limiting REDUCTION to a single shared slope, and that shared slope equals the source's reported
slope only when SPECTRA's scaling fit is run with the same link, weighting, and aggregation (micro vs
macro) as the source's procedure. We therefore define the slope collapse OPERATIONALLY -- re-run the
source's exact slope-fitting procedure on the raw reconstructed pooled accuracies -- and verify agreement
empirically, rather than asserting a 1e-6 algebraic identity for the slope. Subject to that, SPECTRA
strictly dominates the pooled verdict: it reproduces the reported pooled metric exactly and adds the
per-phenomenon resolution on top.

### 2.6 Novelty wedge C1-C4

- C1 (layer): SPECTRA operates only at the diagnostic / evaluation-analysis layer, post-hoc on the
  benchmark's own per-example correctness {y_i}. It adds zero model training and, given the per-example
  prediction logs an evaluation already produces, zero additional inference; if such logs are unavailable,
  predictions are regenerated once at inference cost but still with no training.
- C2 (observable covariate): it conditions on an observable, leakage-free per-example negation-phenomenon
  covariate (cue/scope type derived from the input text alone, via existing markup, rules, or a parser)
  plus a per-stratum reliability covariate r_k.
- C3 (structured monotone partial pooling): it estimates a structured, monotone, hierarchically
  partially-pooled empirical-Bayes field -- not a single global scalar (the pooled metric) and not the
  unpooled noisy per-phenomenon accuracies.
- C4 (provable, contracted exact collapse): it ships the SHRUNK partially-pooled field as the analysis
  product while guaranteeing, through an explicit unit-tested contract, that the source's reported pooled
  gauge is recovered exactly from separately-retained raw materials (E_collapse <= 1e-6). The novelty is
  not the algebraic reaggregation itself -- any raw table trivially reaggregates -- but binding a
  non-trivially transformed (shrunk) field to the source gauge under a checked do-no-harm contract; in the
  full-pooling limit the inverse-scaling slope reduces to a single shared slope that matches the source's
  slope only under its own slope-fitting procedure (same link, weighting, aggregation), verified empirically
  rather than asserted as an identity (Section 2.5).

The four clauses are load-bearing only in combination: C4 is vacuous without C3 (a raw per-category table
has no shrunk field whose faithfulness must be guaranteed), so the defensible novelty is the conjunction --
fine-grained negation typing (C2) plus empirical-Bayes partial pooling (C3) plus leakage controls and a
falsifier ladder plus an explicit collapse contract (C4), all at the diagnostic layer (C1) -- not any
single clause alone. No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4 (Section 3, Q4 enumerates six near-miss
families and the clauses each one misses).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 3. Novel Research Idea (the "5q")

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can the single pooled per-benchmark score (and single inverse-scaling slope) that current analyses use
to summarize a language model's negation ability be replaced by a per-negation-phenomenon competence
field -- a structured, monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable negation-phenomenon covariate --
that reveals which negation phenomena drive failure and how each scales with model size, while provably
collapsing to the reported pooled metric at its default setting?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Negation is a load-bearing phenomenon for safe, reliable language understanding, and the field's current
verdict on it -- "language models are insensitive to negation, and larger models more so" [1] -- is
delivered as a single number per benchmark and a single slope over scale. That monolithic verdict is
consequential: it shapes which models are trusted in negation-critical settings (clinical documentation,
where "no abnormality detected" and "NAD" must be read as absence [18]; legal, safety, and compliance
text where polarity flips meaning), it motivates expensive mitigation research, and it underwrites broad
claims about the limits of scaling [22, 26, 27]. If that verdict is a pooled artifact -- if "cannot do
negation" actually means "cannot do a specific subset of negation phenomena, while handling others, with
the subset masked by averaging" -- then both the scientific picture and the engineering response are
mis-targeted.

Solving this problem matters to the research community in several concrete ways.

P1. It converts a binary, benchmark-level verdict into an actionable map. Instead of "model m scores
0.6 on negation benchmark B", a reviewer learns "model m handles explicit and morphological negation
well but fails implicit, scoped, and NPI-licensed negation", which directly tells dataset builders what
to collect more of and tells method designers what to fix. This reframes a large negation literature
[2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 15] from a leaderboard of pooled numbers into a phenomenon-resolved
diagnostic.

P2. It corrects (or confirms, with evidence) the headline scaling claim. By resolving the inverse-scaling
trend into per-phenomenon slopes, the work tests whether "larger LMs are more insensitive to negation"
[1] is uniform or a Simpson-style blend in which some phenomena scale inversely and others do not. Either
outcome is valuable: a confirmed-uniform result strengthens the claim; a reversal reframes it. This speaks
directly to the inverse-scaling and U-shaped-scaling discourse [26, 27] and to how the field interprets
instruction-tuning's effect [23, 24, 25] (does FLAN lift negation uniformly, or only specific phenomena?).

P3. It is statistically honest about the hard tail. The phenomena that matter most -- implicit, scoped,
licensed negation -- are exactly the rarest in most benchmarks, so their raw per-type accuracies are
noisy. Partial pooling [32, 33] is the principled remedy: it lets the field report small-stratum
competence with calibrated uncertainty rather than either ignoring the tail (pooling it away) or
over-trusting a handful of examples.

P4. It is free, additive, and host-agnostic. Because SPECTRA is a post-hoc re-analysis of predictions
the benchmark already produces, with exact collapse to the reported number, any prior or future negation
evaluation can adopt it at no modeling cost and with no risk of changing published headline numbers. It
generalizes the evaluation methodology of IRT-style and subpopulation analysis [28, 29, 30, 31] to the
negation typology specifically.

P5. It establishes a reusable template: pooled-verdict-to-partially-pooled-field-with-exact-collapse for
any benchmark whose target ability is a mixture of phenomena. Negation is the ideal first instance
because its typology is rich, linguistically grounded [10, 16, 17], and already partially annotated.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The difficulty is not engineering a stratified report; it is doing so in a way that is statistically
valid on the noisy hard tail, leakage-free, faithful to the source number, and falsifiable. Naive
approaches fail on each count.

O1. Entangled, confounded per-phenomenon optima. Per-phenomenon competence is confounded with example
difficulty, frequency, and surface features (a phenomenon stratum may be hard for reasons other than its
negation type). A naive per-type accuracy attributes all of that to negation. The method must residualize
on observable difficulty / frequency proxies and validate that the recovered structure is negation-driven,
not difficulty-driven (handled by the covariate-scramble placebo and difficulty-matched controls in Q5).

O2. Data poverty and noise on the tail. The phenomena of greatest interest are the rarest, so raw
per-stratum accuracies have high variance; a naive unpooled breakdown will manufacture spurious extreme
strata (both spuriously hard and spuriously easy) purely from sampling noise. This is why partial pooling
is mandatory rather than optional, and why the reliability covariate r_k and the monotone shrinkage
lambda(r_k) are central, not cosmetic.

O3. Leakage-free, reproducible covariate. The phenomenon typing must depend only on the input form, never
on the gold label or on the model's correctness, or the diagnostic becomes circular (a stratum defined by
where the model fails will trivially look hard). Automated cue/scope detection is itself imperfect [14],
so the typing's error must be measured and propagated, and a gold-annotated subset used to bound it.

O4. Multi-axis heterogeneity. Negation varies along several partly-correlated axes at once (cue type,
scope length, licensing context, composition with quantifiers/gradables/downward-entailment). A single
flat partition is a lossy proxy; the strata design must capture the dominant axes without exploding the
number of tiny strata (which O2 then has to rescue).

O5. Simultaneous lift and exact collapse. The field must add genuine resolution AND reproduce the source
pooled number bit-for-bit. These pull in opposite directions: any estimator that "improves" the pooled
number has broken faithfulness; any estimator that merely reproduces it has added nothing. SPECTRA resolves
this only because the collapse is computed from retained raw materials while the resolution lives in a
separate shrunk field -- a design constraint, not an afterthought.

O6 (binding). Metric blindness. As argued in Section 2.4, the pooled accuracy and single slope, and the
pooled bootstrap built on them, cannot see the per-phenomenon cancellation; the very instrument used to
judge negation hides the gap. Overcoming this requires phenomenon-stratified evaluation plus falsifiers
(homogeneous-null, monotone-dose, covariate-scramble) and a non-circular downstream validation -- machinery
the pooled paradigm does not contain. This is why straightforward "just report per-category accuracy"
attempts, which exist as appendix tables, have neither dislodged the monolithic verdict nor been trusted
on the tail: they lack the partial pooling (O2), the leakage discipline (O3), the exact-collapse anchor
(O5), and the falsifier ladder (O6) together.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The ingredients exist separately; no prior line combines them, and the field's habits actively discouraged
the combination. We position SPECTRA against six near-miss families and show each misses at least two of
the wedge clauses C1-C4 (Section 2.6).

NM1 -- The source paper and pooled negation probes [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] (sharpest). These deliver the pooled
per-benchmark verdict and the single inverse-scaling slope; some include appendix per-category splits, but
as raw, unpooled tables, with no shrinkage of small strata, no field/mixture estimation, and no
per-phenomenon scaling model. Such a raw table could of course be reaggregated to the pooled micro-accuracy
(reaggregation is trivial), but that is not what C4 contributes: it covers C1 (a diagnostic layer) and
arguably touches C2 (some category splits), yet it misses C3 (no partially-pooled field), and because the
C4 contract binds a SHRUNK field to the gauge, C4 is vacuous here (there is no transformed field whose
faithfulness must be guaranteed). At most two clauses.

NM2 -- Fine-grained / typed negation benchmarks [10, 11, 12, 13, 15] and minimal-pair / NPI suites
[16, 17]. These construct typed negation examples and report raw per-type accuracy. They provide the
typology but as dataset design, not as a post-hoc estimator over an existing pooled metric: no empirical-Bayes
shrinkage of small types (C3), no exact collapse to a host benchmark's reported number (C4), and the typing
is baked into data construction rather than applied as an observable covariate to re-resolve prior pooled
results. They cover C1 and C2 at most: no empirical-Bayes shrinkage of small types (miss C3), and with no
shrunk field the C4 collapse contract is again vacuous (miss C4).

NM3 -- IRT and item-informativeness evaluation [30, 31]. These bring latent-ability machinery and partial
pooling (C3-adjacent), but estimate a single latent ability / item-difficulty pooled over phenomena, not a
per-negation-phenomenon competence field, and carry no exact collapse to a negation gauge. Miss C2
(negation-typed field) and C4.

NM4 -- Subpopulation / behavioral / worst-group evaluation [28, 29]. CheckList and Group-DRO-style analyses
measure targeted slices, but with raw bins (no empirical-Bayes pooling of small slices toward a global
prior), domain-general rather than negation-typed, and no explicit collapse contract binding a shrunk
transformed field to a negation pooled gauge. Miss C3 and C4.

NM5 -- Affirmative-interpretation, meaning-text, and corpus-analysis work on negation [6, 8, 9].
These analyze negation's representation in corpora, propose leveraging affirmative interpretations,
or teach models meaning-text correspondence to better encode negation and antonymy [6]; but they
characterize data or propose a training method; they are not a partially-pooled per-phenomenon
competence estimator over an existing pooled benchmark metric with exact collapse. Miss C2, C3, C4
(as a diagnostic of model competence).

NM6 -- Scaling-law and inverse-scaling analyses [22, 26, 27]. These fit pooled curves over tasks and surface
inverse / U-shaped trends, but do not resolve per-phenomenon scaling within negation with partial pooling,
and have no matched-procedure source-slope verification guard. Miss C2, C3, C4.

The barrier, stated plainly: negation competence was framed as a single benchmark verdict, so per-phenomenon
breakdowns, when produced at all, were raw appendix tables that the noisy tail made easy to distrust and easy
to pool away; the inverse-scaling claim was a single pooled slope; and the linguistic typology of negation
(the raw material for the strata, enumerated in the source's own Introduction [1, 10, 16, 17]) was used to
BUILD diverse benchmarks but never repurposed as a post-hoc covariate to RESOLVE an existing pooled gauge
into a partially-pooled field. To our knowledge, lift (which phenomena fail and how each scales),
statistical validity on the tail (partial pooling), leakage discipline, and exact collapse to the reported
number were never co-targeted. SPECTRA differs by doing all four at once at the diagnostic layer, at zero
model cost.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method. As specified in Section 2.5: (1) type each example into a negation-phenomenon stratum k from an
observable, leakage-free covariate (gold cue/scope annotations where present [10, 11, 12, 13, 15];
otherwise rule-based detection or a negation parser [14], with a gold-annotated audit subset to bound
typing error); (2) compute raw per-stratum accuracy c_k_raw and reliability r_k; (3) form the
empirical-Bayes shrunk field c_k_tilde = lambda(r_k) c_k_raw + (1 - lambda(r_k)) c_global with monotone
lambda(r_k) = tau^2 / (tau^2 + var_k), tau^2 by marginal maximum likelihood (default: one shared map;
optional: phenomenon-family bin maps) [32, 33]; (4) fit the hierarchical per-phenomenon scaling model
beta_k ~ N(beta_global, omega^2); (5) report the competence profile, hard-phenomenon mass M_gamma (vs a
homogeneous-competence permutation null), the scaling-sign map, and the optional Negation Evaluability
Index. Retain {y_i} and {c_k_raw} so that A_pool over the raw materials reproduces A_pool_reported with
E_collapse <= 1e-6 (an exact identity on the pooled metric), while the omega -> 0 limit reduces to the
source's single-slope fit under matched link and aggregation (Section 2.5).

Datasets. (i) The source benchmark suite [1] across its three categories: the negated-cloze / factual
setting (negated LAMA in the lineage of [2, 3], the MKR-NQ / negated-query setting [5]); lexical
antonym/synonym classification; and NLI with negation. (ii) Fine-grained negation benchmarks for richer,
already-typed strata: MoNLI [10], CONDAQA [11], ScoNe [13], NaN-NLI [15], and the large
"This is not a Dataset" benchmark [12], plus NPI / minimal-pair material [16, 17]. In every case the
phenomenon covariate is derived from input-only features (cue/scope markup over the premise/hypothesis or
query text), never from the task gold label or the model's correctness; where a benchmark lacks cue/scope
markup we apply rule-based or parser typing [14] and bound its error against a gold-annotated audit subset
(Q3-O3). (iii) An OPTIONAL clinical external-validity study (outside the core collapse partition): NegEx
[18] is a negation-detection ALGORITHM, not a corpus, so we use NegEx-style rule typing to derive
input-only phenomenon labels on whatever de-identified, properly licensed clinical negation benchmark is
accessible under its data-use agreement, testing whether a model judged "competent at negation" by a
pooled score fails the domain where negation is most safety-critical; if no suitably licensed clinical set
is available this study is dropped without affecting the core result. (iv) Semi-synthetic controlled
mixtures with KNOWN per-phenomenon competence and KNOWN per-phenomenon scaling slope: the mixtures simulate
per-example correctness draws from a specified per-stratum success probability and a specified per-stratum
size-slope (so the ground-truth competence field IS the data-generating parameter, not anything read off a
model), then mix at controlled spreads -- providing non-circular ground truth for recovery and driving the
monotone-dose and homogeneous-null falsifiers.

Models. Reuse existing predictions (no retraining) across a clean size sweep for scaling -- the Pythia
suite [21] and other open auto-regressive families -- plus larger and instruction-tuned models matching
the source's setting (GPT-3 / InstructGPT [19, 25], PaLM / FLAN-PaLM and FLAN-T5 [20, 23, 24]) to test
P2 and the instruction-tuning question.

Baselines. B1 source pooled metric + single slope (the collapse target / identity anchor); B2 raw unpooled
per-type breakdown (the appendix-table baseline; overfits the tail); B3 fixed-bin breakdown without
empirical-Bayes shrinkage; B4 covariate-scrambled typing (placebo: permute phenomenon labels across
examples); B5 single global latent-ability IRT [30] (no phenomenon field); B6 oracle field (known
per-type competence, semi-synthetic upper bound); B7 SPECTRA.

Metrics. PRIMARY (semi-synthetic, where ground truth exists): per-phenomenon competence recovery error
(SPECTRA vs B2/B3) and hard-phenomenon detection AUROC / AUPRC, plus per-phenomenon scaling-sign recovery
accuracy. PRIMARY (real data): a non-circular downstream validation by cross-fitting -- identify the
hard / inverse-scaling phenomena on model family A and split A, then verify on DISJOINT items and a
DIFFERENT model family B that those same phenomena are hardest and show the same scaling sign, compared
against difficulty-matched control strata (so the recovered structure is shown to generalize and to be
negation-driven, not a fit to one model on one split). SECONDARY: variance reduction of partial pooling
vs raw (B7 vs B2) on tail strata; typing-error sensitivity (gold vs rule vs parser); calibration of M_gamma
against the permutation null; and the GUARDRAIL E_collapse <= 1e-6 with the source pooled METRIC reproduced
exactly, while slope agreement is verified separately by re-running the source's slope-fitting procedure on
the reconstructed pooled accuracies under matched link, weighting, and aggregation.

Ablations. A1 typing source (gold cue/scope vs rules vs parser); A2 number / granularity of strata K;
A3 pooling strength (tau^2 estimation; full vs partial vs no pooling limits); A4 reliability-covariate
choice (count vs Wilson vs Beta-Binomial variance); A5 threshold gamma for M_gamma; A6 hierarchical
scaling-variance omega and the random-slope link.

Falsifiers. T1 homogeneous-null: on a benchmark with a single negation type or a uniform injected
competence field, SPECTRA must collapse to the pooled verdict and must NOT manufacture hard-phenomenon mass
beyond the permutation-null rate (this is distinct from the algebraic exact-collapse identity, which holds
on every benchmark). T2 monotone-dose: detection power and recovery accuracy must increase monotonically
with the injected per-phenomenon competence (and slope) spread. T3 covariate-scramble: permuting phenomenon
labels (B4) must destroy the recovered structure; if scrambled typing "explains" the same variance, the
signal is an artifact.

Expected outcomes and hypotheses (numeric ranges are conjectured, with no preliminary results).
H1: the pooled "insensitive to negation" verdict [1] decomposes into a competent-explicit / failing-implicit-
scoped-licensed mixture on several benchmarks, with the hard tail carrying disproportionate weight.
H2 (Simpson test): the pooled inverse-scaling slope is non-uniform across phenomena; on at least one
benchmark a subset of phenomena (for example factual negated cloze) carries the inverse trend while others
are flat or positively scaling, partially reversing the monolithic reading. H3: empirical-Bayes partial
pooling reduces tail-stratum estimate variance by roughly 30-60% relative to raw breakdowns (B2) at matched
coverage. H4: covariate-scramble (T3) erases at least 80% of the recovered hard-phenomenon structure,
establishing that it is negation-driven. H5: instruction tuning [23, 24, 25] lifts specific phenomena
(notably explicit and lexical negation) more than implicit / scoped / licensed negation, so its benefit is
non-uniform -- refining the "instruction tuning reverses inverse scaling" reading [27]. H6: the pooled-metric
identity E_collapse <= 1e-6 holds by construction (verifiable, not merely expected), while slope agreement
with the source is verified empirically under the source's matched slope-fitting procedure rather than
claimed as a by-construction identity.

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations. F1 (data minimization / sensitive content): the optional
clinical study uses only a properly licensed, de-identified clinical benchmark/corpus, typed with
NegEx-style rules [18] (NegEx is an algorithm, not a dataset);
the typing covariate is a function of linguistic form, not author identity; no sensitive content is logged
beyond aggregate per-stratum counts. F2 (fairness of the verdict): the central fairness contribution is to
prevent a pooled score from certifying a model as "good at negation" while it fails a negation phenomenon
that matters in a high-stakes domain; results are reported per phenomenon AND per domain (clinical, legal,
general) so the hard tail cannot be averaged away, and the phenomenon covariate is audited so it does not
become a proxy for dialect, language, or register over-flagging. F3 (do-no-harm collapse default): the
single-bin / full-pooling configuration is an exact disable switch that reproduces the original reported
pooled metric byte-for-byte (E_collapse <= 1e-6), so SPECTRA is shadow-reportable alongside any existing
leaderboard and never silently changes a published number; it is an additive overlay, never a replacement. F4 (scientific
integrity): all numeric hypothesis ranges are marked conjectured; the exact-collapse unit test, the
semi-synthetic generators, the phenomenon-typing rules, and the gold-typing audit subset are released;
because automated cue/scope detection is imperfect [14], phenomenon typing is validated by human linguistic
annotation on a sample and its error propagated into the uncertainty on c_k_tilde. F5 (governance /
auditability): the phenomenon typing, the strata design, gamma, K, tau^2, and the covariate estimator are
documented and versioned, because "LLMs cannot do negation" is a consequential claim that influences model
deployment in safety-critical settings -- so the resolved per-phenomenon picture, with its uncertainty,
must be reported rather than a single number.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
